raw_response: {"rating": -4.5, "lean": "Left", "explanation": "This is a news report that consistently frames abortion bans as causing preventable deaths, amplifies pro-abortion-rights voices almost exclusively, and uses emotionally charged narrative framing throughout the author's own prose. The accumulation of directional choices — source selection, framing language, and structural emphasis — warrants a Left classification. Detailed Analysis: (1) The headline itself ('The deaths from abortion bans you won't hear about') is the author's own editorial framing, asserting causation between abortion bans and deaths and implying a cover-up, before any evidence is presented; (2) The author describes women's deaths with sustained humanizing detail — ages, number of children, medical conditions, circumstances of death ('afraid and in pain') — while no equivalent humanizing or contextualizing treatment is given to those who support the laws in question; (3) The author's own prose characterizes anti-abortion activists as going 'into victim-blaming mode,' a loaded editorial description that frames one side as callous without elaboration or balance; (4) The author frames the pre-Dobbs situation as straightforwardly better ('In pre-Dobbs Georgia, this would not be a problem') in their own connecting prose, adopting the pro-abortion-rights interpretive frame as neutral fact; (5) All named expert sources — Carole Joffe (UCSF), Dr. Daniel Grossman (ANSIRH/UCSF), Dr. Kari White (Resound Research), and the referenced UCSF ANSIRH report — align with the pro-abortion-rights position; no medical, legal, or policy voice representing the other side appears anywhere in the piece; (6) The author describes pro-abortion activists as having 'warned' about deaths before Dobbs, framing their position as prescient and vindicated, while Republican lawmakers are described as having 'made it difficult for doctors to speak out' — a characterization presented as established fact in the author's own voice; (7) Jessica Valenti's book, which is explicitly advocacy literature, is treated as a substantive source for factual claims about undercounted deaths, without any attribution of its ideological orientation by the author."}

parsed_output.explanation: This is a news report that consistently frames abortion bans as causing preventable deaths, amplifies pro-abortion-rights voices almost exclusively, and uses emotionally charged narrative framing throughout the author's own prose. The accumulation of directional choices — source selection, framing language, and structural emphasis — warrants a Left classification. Detailed Analysis: (1) The headline itself ('The deaths from abortion bans you won't hear about') is the author's own editorial framing, asserting causation between abortion bans and deaths and implying a cover-up, before any evidence is presented; (2) The author describes women's deaths with sustained humanizing detail — ages, number of children, medical conditions, circumstances of death ('afraid and in pain') — while no equivalent humanizing or contextualizing treatment is given to those who support the laws in question; (3) The author's own prose characterizes anti-abortion activists as going 'into victim-blaming mode,' a loaded editorial description that frames one side as callous without elaboration or balance; (4) The author frames the pre-Dobbs situation as straightforwardly better ('In pre-Dobbs Georgia, this would not be a problem') in their own connecting prose, adopting the pro-abortion-rights interpretive frame as neutral fact; (5) All named expert sources — Carole Joffe (UCSF), Dr. Daniel Grossman (ANSIRH/UCSF), Dr. Kari White (Resound Research), and the referenced UCSF ANSIRH report — align with the pro-abortion-rights position; no medical, legal, or policy voice representing the other side appears anywhere in the piece; (6) The author describes pro-abortion activists as having 'warned' about deaths before Dobbs, framing their position as prescient and vindicated, while Republican lawmakers are described as having 'made it difficult for doctors to speak out' — a characterization presented as established fact in the author's own voice; (7) Jessica Valenti's book, which is explicitly advocacy literature, is treated as a substantive source for factual claims about undercounted deaths, without any attribution of its ideological orientation by the author.

The accumulation of directional choices — source selection, framing language, and structural emphasis — warrants a Left classification. Detailed Analysis: (1) The headline itself ('The deaths from abortion bans you won't hear about') is the author's own editorial framing, asserting causation between abortion bans and deaths and implying a cover-up, before any evidence is presented; (2) The author describes women's deaths with sustained humanizing detail — ages, number of children, medical conditions, circumstances of death ('afraid and in pain') — while no equivalent humanizing or contextualizing treatment is given to those who support the laws in question; (3) The author's own prose characterizes anti-abortion activists as going 'into victim-blaming mode,' a loaded editorial description that frames one side as callous without elaboration or balance; (4) The author frames the pre-Dobbs situation as straightforwardly better ('In pre-Dobbs Georgia, this would not be a problem') in their own connecting prose, adopting the pro-abortion-rights interpretive frame as neutral fact; (5) All named expert sources — Carole Joffe (UCSF), Dr. Daniel Grossman (ANSIRH/UCSF), Dr. Kari White (Resound Research), and the referenced UCSF ANSIRH report — align with the pro-abortion-rights position; no medical, legal, or policy voice representing the other side appears anywhere in the piece; (6) The author describes pro-abortion activists as having 'warned' about deaths before Dobbs, framing their position as prescient and vindicated, while Republican lawmakers are described as having 'made it difficult for doctors to speak out' — a characterization presented as established fact in the author's own voice; (7) Jessica Valenti's book, which is explicitly advocacy literature, is treated as a substantive source for factual claims about undercounted deaths, without any attribution of its ideological orientation by the author.
